title: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Colony Ship as an 'isometric, party-based RPG' with turn-based play, a detailed skill-based character system, multiple ways to handle quests, branching dialogue trees, and choices-and-consequences faction negotiation aboard a generation ship. That combination strongly fits a party-based CRPG: isometric perspective, party control, authored branching quest structure, tactical/methodical turn-based encounters, and heavy dialogue-choice driven progression. Secondary overlap with western_narrative_rpg and tactical_rpg is present, but the party-based isometric CRPG identity is the clearest core gameplay profile.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: From the same studio, with a dense skill-based CRPG structure focused on multiple quest solutions, consequential choices, and punishing turn-based combat.
Party-based isometric RPG with turn-based tactical encounters, faction politics, dialogue checks, and quest outcomes shaped by player decisions.
Sci-fi isometric CRPG built around exploration, build-driven skill checks, branching quests, and tactical turn-based battles.
Story-heavy isometric RPG with squad-based turn-based combat, conversation choices, and campaign progression through hubs and missions.
Hardcore isometric RPG emphasizing character builds, non-linear questing, difficult tactical combat, and survival in a hostile enclosed world.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Terra Invicta as a long-form strategic campaign centered on faction-level control of Earth's nations, covert political influence, global research direction, proxy conflicts, Solar System expansion, and base/station construction. The text repeatedly emphasizes geopolitics as a sandbox, detailed regional simulation, councilor management, control points, colonization of asteroids/moons/planets, and tactical fleet combat. That macro-scale political and imperial management profile fits grand_strategy best, with strong 4X overlap from expansion, resource extraction, research, and space colonization. Evidence is rich and concrete across Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic-derived stored text, not merely genre tags.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest overall fit: pausable macro-scale sci-fi strategy with research, diplomacy, expansion, colony/station management, and fleet warfare driving a long campaign.

Strong space 4X match through faction asymmetry, colonization, tech progression, empire management, and large-scale fleet conflict, though it is more classical turn-based 4X than Terra Invicta’s geopolitical simulation.

A credible space-strategy neighbor with research, diplomacy, colonization, and military buildup, but it is a more traditional 4X empire game and less distinctive in political infiltration and Earth-to-Solar-System progression.
Pausable macro strategy focused on ideology, geopolitics, research, military buildup, and steering nations through a global existential conflict.
Factional grand strategy driven by character operatives, indirect control, intrigue, diplomacy, and long-form political manipulation rather than direct empire painting.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a spin-off expansion built in 'authentic Arma 3 style,' centered on a military science-fiction campaign with combat recon, electronic warfare, new factions, weapons, vehicles, and a large sandbox terrain suited for combined arms warfare. Store labels also confirm single-player, co-op, and PvP support. The strongest gameplay signals are tactical realism, combat-focused military operations, open sandbox terrain, and methodical shooter play, which fit tactical_shooter best; military_fps is a close secondary fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Contact directly inherits Arma 3’s milsim gunplay, large-map combined-arms sandbox, vehicles, recon, and mission structure.
Another Bohemia tactical military sandbox focused on realistic infantry combat, vehicles, large terrain, and simulation-heavy field operations.
Shares realism-minded infantry combat, coordination, long sightlines, vehicles, and tactical pacing, though it is more multiplayer-session driven.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This is additive DLC for RimWorld and does not contradict the parent game's core identity, so the parent gameplay should drive classification. Parent text explicitly describes RimWorld as a 'sci-fi colony sim' where you manage colonists' needs and relationships, build and craft a settlement, trade, tame animals, form caravans, and respond to raids and other systemic threats. Royalty adds quests, psychic powers, noble titles, throne rooms, hospitality scenarios, and more combat/event variety, but these extend the same colony-management and story-generation loop rather than establishing a different genre. The combined evidence strongly supports colony_sim with systemic sandbox management, emergent narrative, settlement building, colony growth, and tactical encounter handling.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Royalty directly extends RimWorld’s colony sim sandbox, adding quests, nobles, psychic powers, and new tactical options onto the same core survival-management loop.
Deep colony simulation focused on emergent stories, individual pawns with needs and traits, base construction, and crisis-driven survival.
Small-group sci-fi survival colony sim with survivor management, tech progression, base building, and defending against external threats.
Pawns-with-needs colony management, construction, production chains, and raid defense in a sandbox that feels structurally close to RimWorld.
Top-down management sim centered on building layouts, staffing, scheduling, and handling emergent incidents, though with less expedition/combat emphasis.

title: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as grand_strategy using DLC inheritance from the parent game Total War: THREE KINGDOMS, since Eight Princes is clearly an additive chapter pack rather than a genre-shifting mode. The DLC text repeatedly describes new campaign mechanics, princes, buildings, assignments, court options, faction advancement, alignments, and elite units, which matches the parent game's core loop. Parent evidence is strong and explicit: THREE KINGDOMS combines a turn-based campaign of empire-building, statecraft, and conquest with real-time battles. That supports grand_strategy as the primary identity, with rts as a secondary flavor for battlefield engagements. Historical setting, long-form campaign structure, systemic faction management, settlement development, and tactical optimization are all directly supported by the stored descriptions.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Eight Princes is additive DLC built directly on the base game's turn-based campaign, diplomacy, characters, and real-time battles.
Another historical Total War with grand strategy on the campaign map and real-time army battles, plus heavy faction management and political pressure.
Shares the same Total War loop of campaign-map expansion and real-time battles, with stronger faction asymmetry and bespoke campaign mechanics.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Total War Saga: Thrones of Britannia
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Total War Saga: Thrones of Britannia
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A more focused historical Total War entry with similar strategic map play, settlement development, faction progression, and real-time battles.

unresolved_similar_games: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Battle Cry of Freedom as a large-scale multiplayer PvP combat game centered on historically grounded Civil War battles with first- and third-person gunplay, artillery, cavalry, engineer support, melee bayonet/sabre fighting, realistic projectile physics, destructible battlefields, and commander-led tactical play. The strongest identity is tactical shooter: match-based battlefield engagements, realism-leaning weapon behavior, role-based military units, and emphasis on tactical cunning over narrative or RPG progression. It also overlaps with military_fps due to historical armed combat and firearm focus, but the scale, role variety, and tactical realism make tactical_shooter the better primary fit. Evidence for MMO-like scale exists in store genres and 500-600 player battles, but not enough to justify an MMO RPG archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Comparable combined-arms medieval battles, mounted combat, and skill-based melee, though it is less multiplayer-line-battle focused.
Historical realism shooter built around slower lethal gunplay, squad roles, and tactical frontline battles rather than arcade FPS pacing.

title: AI War 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify AI War 2 as a 'grand strategy/RTS hybrid' centered on galactic-scale territorial expansion, fleet and defense-network management, multiple factions with unique economies and rules, and real-time simulation with pause/speed controls. The core loop is long-form campaign play where the player starts from a tiny foothold, expands carefully, manages fronts, optimizes empire and fleet composition, and eventually strikes key AI leaders. The text repeatedly emphasizes macro decision-making, automation, tactical optimization, and large-scale systemic simulation rather than match-based or action-oriented play, which strongly fits grand_strategy with RTS and 4X overlap.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares the galactic strategy layer, multi-faction simulation, and long-form empire planning, though it leans more grand strategy than RTS.
A complex pausable real-time space grand strategy game with heavy automation, sprawling simulation, and large-scale strategic decision-making.

unresolved_similar_games: Sins of a Solar Empire: Rebellion

title: Killer7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Killer7 as a single-player, story-driven action-adventure with a deeply authored linear campaign, a dark surreal tone, puzzle-solving, character-switching, and hybrid first/third-person shooting encounters. The evidence clearly supports linear progression, encounter-based ranged combat, environmental/puzzle elements, and a strong authored narrative. It does not cleanly match shooter-specialist archetypes like military_fps, tactical_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter, and the horror is present more as tone and imagery than as pure survival-horror structure, so cinematic_action_adventure is the best fit with action_horror as a secondary influence.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Shadows of the Damned: Hella Remastered
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Suda-directed linear action-horror shooter with grotesque surrealism, puzzle gates, and a heavily authored campaign.
Linear encounter-driven action-horror campaign mixing shooting, exploration, and light environmental puzzles.
Suda-style assassin action with strong auteur tone, stylized presentation, and boss-focused single-player progression.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: El Shaddai: Ascension of the Metatron
staged_neighbors.requested_title: El Shaddai: Ascension of the Metatron
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Highly stylized linear action-adventure emphasizing surreal presentation, authored pacing, and unconventional storytelling.

unresolved_similar_games: The House of the Dead: Overkill

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently depict a single-player, third-person 3D open-world survival adventure centered on exploring an alien planet, evading a world-destroying cylinder, solving environmental puzzles, and adapting a herd via mutations that unlock traversal and survival abilities. The core loop is exploration, survival under pressure, puzzle-solving, and story progression rather than shooter combat or RPG party tactics. Repeated mentions of a procedurally generated ecosystem, emergent gameplay, real-time world destruction, rescuing and growing the herd, and mutation-based adaptation strongly support exploration_survival_adventure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on third-person survival exploration, vulnerable-creature fantasy, clan/herd growth, and evolution-based progression through a dangerous world, though it is more systemic survival sim and less authored puzzle-adventure.

Strong overlap in being a fragile creature navigating a hostile ecosystem through evasion, food/shelter survival, environmental learning, and non-dominant combat, but its 2D survival-platformer structure is a clear step away from the anchor's 3D open-world mutation adventure.

Good fit for leading small creatures with differentiated abilities through exploration and environmental obstacles on an alien world, but its core identity is squad-command strategy/puzzle play rather than survival adaptation.
Centers on vulnerable animal survival, protecting a family group, and traversing an open natural world under persistent threat.
Matches creature-group problem solving and exploration-gated progression, though its structure is more directed and less survival-focused.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Juno: New Origins as a 3D aerospace sandbox centered on constructing and testing rockets, planes, cars, and planets with realistic physics. The core loop is design/build/test/iterate, with deep vehicle customization, orbital mechanics, automation scripting, persistent sandbox play, and a career layer of contracts, milestones, money, and tech-tree unlocks. There is no combat emphasis; the challenge comes from simulation realism and engineering optimization. This strongly fits engineering_sandbox_sim.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest gameplay match: build spacecraft from parts, manage staging and orbital physics, and play through sandbox/science/career-style progression with mission-driven space program simulation.
Very similar build-test-iterate engineering loop with realistic flight modeling and deep custom vehicle creation, but it is focused on aircraft more than full aerospace/orbital sandbox progression.

Strong engineering and construction sandbox overlap with ship design and physics-driven testing, but survival, mining, destructibility, and combat pull it away from Juno’s aerospace-program and orbital-sim identity.
Rocket design, orbital flight, mission planning, and iteration around realistic-ish aerospace physics.
Creative engineering sandbox built around constructing programmable vehicles and refining their performance through testing.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify Paper Dolls 2 as a first-person horror adventure set in a haunted Qing-dynasty mansion, centered on exploration, unraveling a buried mystery, solving puzzles, collecting clues/messages, and surviving or avoiding hostile spirits. The copy explicitly says players must 'avoid, shake off, and defeat' lost souls, which supports some light survival-horror interaction, but combat specifics are sparse and do not clearly establish action-forward combat. The strongest identity is therefore psychological horror: first-person, single-player, authored campaign, oppressive haunted-house exploration, mystery-solving, and survival-escape framing, with survival_horror as a secondary influence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on first-person haunted-house exploration, clue-driven progression, puzzle solving, and oppressive psychological horror structure; lighter on explicit combat, but strongly aligned overall.
First-person horror centered on story, puzzles, investigation, and stealthy evasion of spirits is a strong gameplay match, even if it leans more purely evasive than the anchor.

Good fit on first-person survival horror, authored interior exploration, stealth under threat, and puzzle-gated progress, though its enemy model and defensive tool push it slightly farther from the anchor's ghostly adventure feel.
Direct predecessor with the same first-person haunted-mansion exploration, puzzle solving, spirit evasion, and Chinese supernatural horror identity.
First-person East Asian psychological horror focused on claustrophobic interior exploration, environmental storytelling, puzzles, and oppressive supernatural tension.

removed_candidates: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Official store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify Amazing Cultivation Simulator as a strategy sim/management game inspired by RimWorld and Dwarf Fortress. Core play centers on designing and expanding a cultivation sect, managing disciples, researching knowledge, building infrastructure, handling commerce/political relations, and surviving a procedurally generated world with changing NPC sect interactions. Combat and threats exist (spirits, monsters, demons, beast sieges), but they support a broader settlement/colony-management loop rather than defining the game as an action RPG. The strongest fit in the taxonomy is colony_sim, with management_tycoon and city_builder as secondary influences.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Colonist management, emergent storytelling, base building, research, raids, and faction/world-map interaction are the clearest gameplay match.
Deep simulation-driven colony management with complex systems, procedural worlds, and emergent disasters is a foundational comparison point.
Build-and-manage a settlement, assign work, defend against threats, and grow a colony through layered sim systems and base planning.
Shares squad management, base expansion, faction politics, training-based progression, and open-world expeditions, though it is more directly character-driven.
Another survival-colony management sim focused on assigning survivors, building up a base, researching systems, and responding to external threats.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Insurgency as a tactical FPS centered on lethal close-quarters firefights, realistic weapon handling, suppression, no crosshair, squad roles, territorial/objective modes, and mission-based cooperative play. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'Tactical FPS,' while the text repeatedly emphasizes competitive multiplayer, co-op objective missions, one-life tension, teamwork, and realistic combat across modern conflict environments in the Middle East, Africa, and Central Asia. This strongly supports tactical_shooter over broader shooter archetypes.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct follow-up with the same lethal tactical gunplay, objective modes, squad roles, and co-op checkpoint structure.
Built on a very similar class-based, low-HUD, one-life/limited-respawn tactical FPS framework, just shifted to a WWII setting.
Another unforgiving objective shooter focused on realistic weapon lethality, territory control, asymmetry, and team coordination.
Shares realism, communication, squad roles, and objective-driven combat, though on a larger-scale milsim battlefield.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Photonmelodies as a compilation of three story-driven Muv-Luv side stories and follow-up scenarios, emphasizing novella-length adventures, character interactions, romance/comedy, lore, and narrative continuation rather than action mechanics. The copy repeatedly frames the experience around 'stories,' 'spinoff title,' 'follow-up,' 'your story to unfold,' and spending one-on-one time with characters. No concrete evidence supports shooter, tactics, or other active gameplay loops in this package, while the franchise presentation here is clearly narrative-first, single-player, casual, and authored. That strongly fits visual_novel.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest fit: another Muv-Luv side-story compilation/fandisc with the same narrative-first anthology structure, familiar cast/worlds, and VN reading loop.
Core Muv-Luv visual novel and direct narrative touchstone for Photonmelodies content; same audience and VN structure, though not an anthology package.
Foundational Muv-Luv VN with the same reading-driven format, character routes, school-life/comedy elements, and franchise storytelling DNA.
Narrative-first route-based VN with romance, slice-of-life, and darker military/conspiracy threads; good audience overlap, but less directly similar in structure and franchise function.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Imperial Capital Burns - Muv-Luv Alternative Total Eclipse
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same franchise VN side story material focused on lore expansion, military cast, and narrative-first progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Garden Paws as a cozy farm-and-town life sim: inheriting a farm, growing crops, raising animals, running a shop, crafting, building out the town, upgrading a home, and completing hundreds of quests across islands and caves. The core loop is persistent lifestyle progression and world improvement rather than combat, with light dungeon monster battles as side content. Strong support exists for life-sim identity through farming, shopkeeping, animal care, customization, housing, town rebuilding, and social questing, with optional online co-op layered on top.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close overall fit: inherited farm, crops, animals, fishing, mining, crafting, town restoration, light combat, and optional co-op all align with Garden Paws' core life-sim loop.
Strong match on quest-driven crafting, gathering, farming, workshop progression, and town rebuilding, though it is more commission/workshop-led and single-player focused than Garden Paws.
Good fit for cozy open-ended town building, farming, gathering, crafting, and multiplayer sandbox progression; survival pressure and hunting/combat are somewhat more prominent than in Garden Paws.
Persistent life sim with farming, exploration, crafting, questing, and multiple player-run shops baked into long-term town life.
Town-focused routine sim with resource gathering, light combat, crafting, and serving locals through a shop-adjacent service loop.

title: Total War: WARHAMMER - Realm of The Wood Elves

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This title is clearly additive DLC for Total War: WARHAMMER and does not contradict the parent game's core gameplay, so parent evidence is primary. The parent description explicitly defines the loop as 'turn-based campaign of epic empire-building with explosive, colossal, real-time battles,' which strongly supports an RTS-primary strategy identity with grand-strategy overlap. The DLC description preserves that structure while adding a new race, campaign map, faction currency, building/wonder victory condition, outposts, tech/building infrastructure, lord/hero skill trees, and large-scale real-time battles with ranged, melee, and magic units. Together this supports a long-form tactical-overhead strategy game centered on real-time command, settlement/infrastructure building, faction growth, and campaign-level conquest.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The required base game; this DLC directly extends its campaign layer, factions, progression, and real-time army battles.
Same Total War: Warhammer hybrid structure of turn-based grand strategy plus real-time battles, with faction-specific campaign mechanics and Legendary Lords.
The closest modern counterpart: identical core campaign-and-battle loop, asymmetric fantasy factions, heroes, magic, and massive tactical clashes.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Total War: Warhammer - Call of the Beastmen
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Total War: WARHAMMER - Call of the Beastmen
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another race-pack DLC for the same base game, adding a distinct faction, lords, units, campaign mechanics, and story-driven campaign content.

unresolved_similar_games: Total War: WARHAMMER II - Rise of the Tomb Kings

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a combat-forward third-person action RPG loop centered on descending into Hell Gate, fighting monsters and hostile humans, collecting randomized loot, crafting gear from materials, storing recovered equipment, and permanently improving via retained masteries across seasonal resets. The corpus explicitly calls out 'Melee Action,' separate left/right-hand weapon use, guard breaks, deathblows, randomized enemy placements and loot, randomized weapon/armor traits, and duo co-op plus PvP. Horror tone and setting are strong, but the core identity is better captured as a loot-driven action RPG rather than a pure horror archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct franchise benchmark with the same tower-run premise, harsh death penalties, scavenged gear, crafting, and online invasion/competitive hooks.
Very close on fast lethal melee action, heavy loot/build systems, repeat runs, and meaningful co-op, though it is more mission-structured and less survival-scavenge focused.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Stranger of Paradise: Final Fantasy Origin
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Stranger of Paradise: Final Fantasy Origin
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A solid loot-heavy hardcore action RPG comp with class builds and online co-op, but its mission-based structure and party/job focus are less like INFERNO's death-run scavenging loop.
Demanding action-RPG combat, dense gear/build progression, harsh boss-driven challenge, and online co-op in a fast-paced melee framework.
Third-person punishing melee with targeted limb-based loot, gear crafting/upgrading, repeated recovery runs, and a hostile labyrinthine world.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify a hybrid of side-scrolling personal combat and strategy/base-building: the player directly fights in 2D battles while issuing troop orders, managing formations, building strongholds, recruiting units, and planning expansion. The Steam detailed copy also establishes a roguelite run structure with branching map progression, loss-on-death resets, and permanent unlocks for future runs. This strongly matches side_scrolling_action_strategy over pure action-platformer, RTS, or management archetypes.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit here: side-scrolling kingdom expansion, recruiting subjects, building defenses, real-time pressure, and ruler presence on the field strongly echo the anchor, though combat is more indirect and tower-defense-like.
Very close on 2D forward expansion, limited-resource kingdom building, and run-based strategic survival, but even more minimalist and less directly combat-driven than Sons of Valhalla.

Good hybrid comp for building a base, balancing economy/defense, recruiting troops, and personally fighting in battles, but its wave-defense structure is more compact and less campaign/roguelite-expedition oriented.

Shares roguelite map pressure, tactical unit composition, and defense/recapture decisions, but it is primarily a real-time tactics game without the anchor’s side-scrolling avatar combat and base-building economy loop.
Blends side-scrolling action with base fortification, unit placement, and defensive preparation between fights.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as an 'action roguelike for 1-4 players' with procedurally generated non-linear dungeons, permadeath, random encounters, many bosses, strong item/weapon synergies, and both solo and up-to-4-player co-op. The core loop is clearly run-based dungeon combat with repeated randomized runs and build variation, which strongly matches co_op_action_roguelite. Combat is dominant and supports melee, magic, and ranged playstyles. Unlockable characters and persistent alternative RPG mode support light metaprogression/buildcraft without changing the main roguelite identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match: 4-player co-op, side-view action, randomized dungeon runs, roguelike structure, loot/build variation, and boss-focused progression align strongly with the anchor.

Strong fit on cooperative procedural runs, class choice, loot, and repeated progression, though its heavier persistent town/meta progression makes it a bit less like Son of a Witch’s more arcade-roguelike run feel.
Good overlap in action roguelike dungeon runs, item/spell synergies, bosses, and co-op support, but it is more focused on tight spell-combo combat and supports smaller co-op scale than the anchor.

Shares procedural runs, unlockable characters, chaotic item interactions, and multiplayer replayability, but its immersive-sim mission structure and open-ended city levels are a looser fit than dungeon-crawl room combat.
1-4 player co-op action roguelite built around fast room-based dungeon runs, bosses, synergies, and repeatable buildcraft.

title: Total War: Warhammer 2 - Rise of the Tomb Kings

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as grand_strategy based on strong official DLC copy describing a long-form faction campaign with race-specific strategic systems: campaign objectives in Eye of the Vortex/Mortal Empires, researching dynasties, unique resources (Canopic Jars), rites, buildings/growth/public order effects, recruitment-cap expansion, quest-like collection of the Books of Nagash, and army management that culminates in major battles. The DLC also explicitly adds battle-layer mechanics such as troop regeneration, summons, artillery, ambush-related sandstorm effects, and unit rosters, which supports RTS as a secondary hybrid identity. Even without parent-game inheritance, the stored text provides concrete strategic loop evidence well beyond title-only metadata.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The parent game and core ruleset this DLC plugs into: identical campaign structure, real-time battles, lord progression, and faction-based expansion.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Total War: WARHAMMER II - Curse of the Vampire Coast
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Total War: WARHAMMER II - Curse of the Vampire Coast
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another Warhammer II race pack built around a bespoke faction economy, unique campaign objectives, new legendary lords, and the same strategic-plus-tactical loop.
Very close gameplay identity: turn-based empire management with large-scale real-time fantasy battles, faction asymmetry, and campaign conquest.

unresolved_similar_games: Total War: WARHAMMER II - The Queen & The Crone
Total War: WARHAMMER III - Champions of Chaos

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify ARC Raiders as a multiplayer extraction adventure / cooperative third-person shooter built around repeated topside raids into distinct maps, scavenging loot, surviving hostile machines and rival players, then returning to an underground hub/workshop to sell, craft, repair, upgrade gear, and improve skills. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvP threat from other Raiders, crafting, workshop progression, quests from traders, skill-tree advancement, and tactical third-person gunplay with gadgets and environmental/physics-based problem solving. That combination strongly matches extraction_shooter over a generic tactical or loot shooter.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Third-person PvPvE extraction looter focused on entering maps, scavenging resources, surviving encounters, and bringing loot home for shelter progression.
High-stakes PvPvE extraction structure with AI threats, rival squads, noisy combat decisions, and survival-based loot extraction.
A core extraction-shooter reference point: raid-based scavenging, persistent gear risk, crafting, quests, and hub-side progression, though in first person.
Third-person co-op shooter with gear grinding, buildcraft, skills, gadgets, and tactical combat; less extraction-focused but close on combat and progression feel.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Battle Brothers as a turn-based tactical RPG centered on leading and managing a mercenary company through an open-world campaign. The text explicitly describes a strategic world map plus a tactical combat layer, freely taking contracts, recruiting and equipping soldiers, and switching into detailed turn-based battles against hostile parties. The corpus strongly supports turn_based_tactics as the primary identity through tactical battles, party management, heavy optimization, injuries/fatigue/morale systems, and long-form campaign structure. Secondary party_based_crpg support exists via recruitment, leveling, equipment management, and company-building, but combat-forward tactical encounters are the clearest core loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on mercenary-band sandbox structure, overworld travel, contracts, recruitment, gear management, and brutal turn-based squad combat.
Strong fit via contract-driven mercenary management, finances, roster progression, travel layer, and deep turn-based tactics, though mission structure and mech focus make it less samey.
Open strategic map, hired mercs, squad-level turn-based combat, equipment/perk management, and multi-party campaign decisions map well, despite a more authored campaign.
Shares harsh low-fantasy survival tactics, procedural campaigning, recruitment, and persistent roster building, but is less centered on contracts, economy, and mercenary-company simulation.
Pairs a strategic management layer with brutal turn-based squad combat, soldier progression, injuries, and permadeath pressure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This title is clearly additive DLC for American Truck Simulator, and its own description focuses on new Colorado roads, cities, landmarks, industries, and cargo routes rather than a different gameplay mode. Parent-game evidence is strong: players drive licensed American trucks, deliver cargo across open road networks, obey traffic rules, manage transmissions/brakes, park long trailers, and grow from driver-for-hire into an owner-operator who expands a trucking company with garages, fleets, and hired drivers. The Colorado DLC preserves that same core loop by adding a new region to explore and haul freight through. That strongly supports transport_sim with open-world road traversal, vehicle control, sim-realism challenge, non-combat play, and company/base growth progression.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Colorado is additive DLC for this base trucking sim and keeps its exact cargo-hauling, road-driving, and company-building gameplay.
Same SCS long-haul truck sim structure with job delivery, realistic road driving, vehicle management, and map-based progression.
Another ATS state expansion adding new roads, cities, industries, and delivery routes to the same core sim.
State DLC for the same game with identical freight-hauling gameplay, progression systems, and exploration-driven map expansion.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently presents Twofold as an interactive story centered on character relationships rather than action systems. OpenCritic labels it 'Interactive Story'; Steam/OpenCritic descriptions focus on Olive navigating a college social conflict, with 'two distinct core paths to travel down' and optional friendship side paths, which strongly indicates a branching narrative structure. The text emphasizes story, characters, voice acting, soundtrack, CG gallery, and accessibility/UI options, while providing no evidence of combat, platforming, RPG systems, or simulation loops. Single-player support is explicitly listed on Steam. Together this strongly fits visual_novel.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another Studio Élan-style narrative VN emphasizing romance, interpersonal drama, and choice-led progression over mechanical systems.
Character-focused visual novel with strong emotional writing, relationship routes, and a gentle slice-of-life reading loop.
Recognizable yuri visual novel comp driven by dialogue, romance, and route-like story progression in a mostly linear interactive format.

title: Mordheim: City of the Damned - Witch Hunters

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive: it adds a new Witch Hunters warband, unit types, and a playable campaign, with no evidence of a different core mode or genre. Parent-game stored descriptions explicitly identify Mordheim: City of the Damned as a turn-based tactical game where you lead warbands into lethal skirmishes, blending RPG progression, unit customization, gear, mission-based battles, and multiplayer. The DLC text reinforces the same warband-based tactical structure through unit classes, weapons, skills, spellcasting, and campaign content. This strongly supports turn_based_tactics as primary, with tactical_rpg as secondary due to unit growth, customization, and RPG progression layers.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: This DLC directly inherits the base game's turn-based warband skirmishes, campaign structure, progression, and injury-permadeath management.
Small-band tactical combat, persistent roster development, equipment management, and punishing long-term losses make it a very close campaign-level match.
Turn-based squad tactics with heavy emphasis on soldier builds, risk management, and campaign consequences between battles.
Another Warhammer turn-based tactics game focused on ability-driven squad composition, mission planning, and progression across a campaign.
Games Workshop skirmish adaptation centered on gang-sized tactical battles, fighter customization, and campaign-style roster advancement.
